html:
I

The Advertisement

On New Year‚Äôs Day, 1943, G. H. Bondy, head of the great Metallo-Electrical Company, was sitting as usual reading his paper. He skipped the news from the theatre of war rather disrespectfully, avoided the Cabinet crisis, then crowded on sail (for the People‚Äôs Journal, which had grown long ago to five times its ancient size, now afforded enough canvas for an ocean voyage) for the Finance and Commerce section. Here he cruised about for quite a while, then furled his sails, and abandoned himself to his thoughts.

‚ÄúThe Coal Crisis!‚Äù he said to himself. ‚ÄúMines getting worked out; the Ostrava basin suspending work for years. Heavens above, it‚Äôs a sheer disaster! We‚Äôll have to import Upper Silesian coal. Just work out what that will add to the cost of our manufactures, and then talk about competition. We‚Äôre in a pretty fix. And if Germany raises her tariff, we may as well shut up shop. And the Industrial Banks going down, too! What a wretched state of affairs! What a hopeless, stupid, stifling state of affairs! Oh, damn the crisis!‚Äù

Here G. H. Bondy, Chairman of the Board of Directors, came to a pause. Something was fidgeting him and would not let him rest. He traced it back to the last page of his discarded newspaper. It was the syllable tion, only part of a word, for the fold of the paper came just in front of the t. It was this very incompleteness which had so curiously impressed itself upon him.

‚ÄúWell, hang it, it‚Äôs probably iron production,‚Äù Bondy pondered vaguely, ‚Äúor prevention, or, maybe, restitution‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And the Azote shares have gone down, too. The stagnation‚Äôs simply shocking. The position‚Äôs so bad that it‚Äôs ridiculous‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ But that‚Äôs nonsense: who would advertise the restitution of anything? More likely resignation. It‚Äôs sure to be resignation.‚Äù

With a touch of annoyance, G. H. Bondy spread out the newspaper to dispose of this irritating word. It had now vanished amid the chequering of the small advertisements. He hunted for it from one column to another, but it had concealed itself with provoking ingenuity. Mr.¬ÝBondy then worked from the bottom up, and finally started again from the right-hand side of the page. The contumacious ‚Äútion‚Äù was not to be found.

Mr.¬ÝBondy did not give in. He refolded the paper along its former creases, and behold, the detestable tion leaped forth on the very edge. Keeping his finger firmly on the spot, he swiftly spread the paper out once more, and found‚ÅÝ‚ÄîMr.¬ÝBondy swore under his breath. It was nothing but a very modest, very commonplace small advertisement:

Invention

Highly remunerative, suitable for any factory, for immediate sale, personal reasons. Apply R. Marek, Engineer, B≈ôevnov, 1651.

‚ÄúSo that‚Äôs all it was!‚Äù thought G. H. Bondy. ‚ÄúSome sort of patent braces; just a cheap swindle or some crazy fellow‚Äôs pet plaything. And here I‚Äôve wasted five minutes on it! I‚Äôm getting scatterbrained myself. What a wretched state of affairs! And not a hint of improvement anywhere!‚Äù

He settled himself in a rocking-chair to savour in more comfort the full bitterness of this wretched state of affairs. True, the M.E.C. had ten factories and 34,000 employees. The M.E.C. was the leading producer of iron. The M.E.C. had no competitor as regards boilers. The M.E.C. grates were world-famous. But after thirty years‚Äô hard work, gracious Heavens, surely one would have got bigger results elsewhere‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

G. H. Bondy sat up with a jerk. ‚ÄúR. Marek, Engineer; R. Marek, Engineer. Half a minute: mightn‚Äôt that be that red-haired Marek‚ÅÝ‚Äîlet‚Äôs see, what was his name? Rudolph, Rudy Marek, my old chum Rudy of the Technical School? Sure enough, here it is in the advertisement: ‚ÄòR. Marek, Engineer.‚Äô Rudy, you rascal, is it possible? Well, you‚Äôve not got on very far in the world, my poor fellow! Selling ‚Äòa highly remunerative invention.‚Äô Ha! ha! ‚Äò‚Ä¶¬Ýfor personal reasons.‚Äô We know all about those ‚Äòpersonal reasons.‚Äô No money, isn‚Äôt that what it is? You want to catch some jay of a manufacturer on a nicely limed ‚Äòpatent,‚Äô do you? Ah, well, you always had rather a notion of turning the world upside down. Ah, my lad, where are all our fine notions now! And those extravagant, romantic days when we were young!‚Äù

Bondy lay back in his chair once more.

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs quite likely it really is Marek,‚Äù he reflected. ‚ÄúStill, Marek had a head for science. He was a bit of a talker, but there was a touch of genius about the lad. He had ideas. In other respects he was a fearfully unpractical fellow. An absolute fool, in fact. It‚Äôs very surprising that he isn‚Äôt a Professor,‚Äù mused Mr.¬ÝBondy. ‚ÄúI haven‚Äôt set eyes on him for twenty years. God knows what he has been up to; perhaps he‚Äôs come right down in the world. Yes, he must be down and out, living away over in B≈ôevnov, poor chap‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and getting a living out of inventions! What an awful finish!‚Äù

He tried to imagine the straits of the fallen inventor. He managed to picture a horribly shaggy and dishevelled head, surrounded by dismal paper walls like those in a film. There is no furniture, only a mattress in the corner, and a pitiful model made of spools, nails, and match-ends on the table. A murky window looks out on a little yard. Upon this scene of unspeakable indigence enters a visitor in rich furs. ‚ÄúI have come to have a look at your invention.‚Äù The half-blind inventor fails to recognize his old schoolfellow. He humbly bows his tousled head, looks about for a seat to offer to his guest, and then, oh Heaven! with his poor, stiff, shaking fingers he tries to get his sorry invention going‚ÅÝ‚Äîit‚Äôs some crazy perpetual motion device‚ÅÝ‚Äîand mumbles confusedly that it should work, and certainly would work, if only he had‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ if only he could buy‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ The fur-coated visitor looks all round the garret, and suddenly he takes a leather wallet from his pocket and lays on the table one, two (Mr.¬ÝBondy takes fright and cries ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs enough!‚Äù) three thousand-crown notes. (‚ÄúOne would have been quite enough‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ to go on with, I mean,‚Äù protests something in Mr.¬ÝBondy‚Äôs brain.)

‚ÄúThere is‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ something to carry on the work with, Mr.¬ÝMarek. No, no, you‚Äôre not in any way indebted to me. Who am I? That doesn‚Äôt matter. Just take it that I am a friend.‚Äù

Bondy found this scene very pleasant and touching.

‚ÄúI‚Äôll send my secretary to Marek,‚Äù he resolved; ‚Äútomorrow without fail. And what shall I do today? It‚Äôs a holiday; I‚Äôm not going to the works. My time‚Äôs my own‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ a wretched state things are in! Nothing to do all day long! Suppose I went round today myself.‚Äù

G. H. Bondy hesitated. It would be a bit of an adventure to go and see for oneself how that queer fellow was struggling along in B≈ôevnov.

‚ÄúAfter all, we were such chums! And old times have their claim on one. Yes, I‚Äôll go!‚Äù decided Mr.¬ÝBondy. And he went.

He had rather a boring time while his car was gliding all over B≈ôevnov in search of a mean hovel bearing the number 1651. They had to inquire at the police-station.

‚ÄúMarek, Marek,‚Äù said the inspector, searching his memory. ‚ÄúThat must be Marek the engineer, of Marek and Co., the electric lamp factory, 1651, Mixa Street.‚Äù

The electric lamp factory! Bondy felt disappointed, even annoyed. Rudy Marek wasn‚Äôt living up in a garret, then! He was a manufacturer and wanted to sell some invention or other ‚Äúfor personal reasons.‚Äù If that didn‚Äôt smell of bankruptcy, his name wasn‚Äôt Bondy.

‚ÄúDo you happen to know how Mr.¬ÝMarek is doing?‚Äù he asked the police inspector, with a casual air, as he took his seat in the car.

‚ÄúOh, splendidly!‚Äù the inspector answered. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs got a very fine business.‚Äù Local pride made him add, ‚ÄúThe firm‚Äôs very well known‚Äù; and he amplified this with: ‚ÄúA very wealthy man, and a learned one, too. He does nothing but make experiments.‚Äù

‚ÄúMixa Street!‚Äù cried Bondy to his chauffeur.

‚ÄúThird on the right!‚Äù the inspector called after the car.

Bondy was soon ringing at the residential part of quite a pretty little factory.

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs all very nice and clean here,‚Äù he remarked to himself. ‚ÄúFlowerbeds in the yard, creeper on the walls. Humph! There always was a touch of the philanthropist and reformer about that confounded Marek.‚Äù And at that moment Marek himself came out on the steps to meet him; Rudy Marek, awfully thin and serious-looking, up in the clouds, so to speak. It gave Bondy a queer pang to find him neither so young as he used to be nor so unkempt as that inventor; so utterly different from what Bondy had imagined that he was scarcely recognizable. But before he could fully realize his disillusionment, Marek stretched out his hand and said quietly, ‚ÄúWell, so you‚Äôve come at last, Bondy! I‚Äôve been expecting you!‚Äù
